description: Documented malicious or compromised Docker Hub images and container supply-chain incidents. Major themes: cryptominer images, typosquats of official images, TeamTNT-era credential-stealer images, and shared-cache layer poisoning.

entries.name: alpine-typosquat-cluster
entries.description: Cluster of Alpine-base typosquats (e.g. `alpinex`, `alp1ne`, `alphine`) carrying reverse shells and cryptominers. Particularly dangerous in CI: dockerfile `FROM alpinex:latest` slips past visual review.

entries.name: docker-build-cache-poisoning-pattern
entries.description: Pattern: Docker Build Cloud / BuildKit shared remote cache, when not scoped per-namespace, allows one tenant's malicious layer to be reused by another tenant's build. Documented as a class of supply-chain attack against shared CI caches.

entries.name: kinsing-xmrig-image-family
entries.description: Kinsing malware family operated several Docker Hub images targeting exposed Docker daemons (port 2375). Combined XMRig mining, AWS credential theft, and lateral movement into Kubernetes.

entries.name: mysqlserver
entries.description: Typosquat of the official `mysql` image. Carried an XMRig miner that auto-started on container run. Pulled 1.4M+ times before removal.

entries.name: nginx-typosquat-image
entries.description: Typosquats of the official `nginx` image (e.g. `nginx-alpine` posted by an unverified publisher, `n1ginx`, `nginxserver`) carrying cryptominers and reverse shells. Triggered Docker Verified Publisher tightening.

entries.name: sysdig-2023-malicious-image-cluster
entries.description: Sysdig 2023 analysis found 1,652 malicious Docker images on Docker Hub: cryptominers, reverse shells, web shells, and TeamTNT-style cloud-credential stealers. Average lifetime ~1 week before takedown.

entries.name: teamtnt-credstealer
entries.description: TeamTNT pushed dozens of Docker images carrying AWS credential stealers (kubelet/kube-config grep), XMRig miners, and lateral-movement tooling. Targeted exposed Docker APIs and misconfigured Kubernetes clusters.

entries.name: unit42-20m-cryptojacking-cluster
entries.description: Cluster of Docker Hub accounts distributing cryptojacking images documented by Palo Alto Unit 42 (~20 million pulls in aggregate). Images install dependencies, clone XMRig from its official GitHub repo, build the Monero miner from source, and run it; some tags bundle alternate miners so the attacker can match the victim's hardware. The shared signal across the family is a hardcoded wallet address / mining-pool credential.
indicators: publisher accounts: azurenql, 021982, dockerxmrig, ggcloud1, ggcloud2
clones and builds XMRig from source at image build/run time
hardcoded Monero wallet address or mining-pool credentials

entries.name: xmrig-cryptominer-cluster
entries.description: Aqua Security found 30+ Docker Hub images pulling XMRig cryptominers on `docker run`. Several were typosquats of `xmrig/xmrig`, `docker`, `ubuntu`, etc. Total downloads in the millions before takedowns.

entries.name: dero-miner-container-worm
entries.description: Kaspersky (Securelist) documented a self-spreading Dero cryptocurrency miner that propagates through Docker daemons exposing the API on port 2375. After compromising a host it runs two UPX-packed Go binaries masquerading as legitimate software: 'nginx' (a propagation/persistence module that uses masscan to find more exposed 2375 endpoints and spawns new ubuntu:18.04 containers with --restart always) and 'cloud' (the Dero miner). It needs no central C2, making the worm resilient; the same wallet/node infrastructure was reused from 2023-2024 Kubernetes cryptojacking campaigns.

entries.name: kazutod-tene-ten-resource-hijack
entries.description: Darktrace and Cado Security Labs (April 2025) analysed the Docker Hub image kazutod/tene:ten, which runs a Python script ten.py hidden in the image layers behind multi-layer obfuscation. Rather than mining with XMRig, the de-obfuscated payload connects to teneo.pro (a Web3 data-network startup) to farm reward points by faking heartbeats — a resource-/account-abuse twist on cryptojacking.
indicators: embedded obfuscated ten.py in image layers
connects to teneo.pro to farm reward points (not direct coin mining)
